coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive and portable fluorescence polyurethane indicator paper for real-time, visual and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Shan, GAO
Li, WANG
Zhijia, ZHANG
Rui, LI
Qiang, WANG
Guojun, WANG
Hao, WEI
Xiao, OUYANG
coredata.dc:description: Visual and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) for public and environmental protection is an extremely urgent issue and challenge. Herein, a novel fluorescence polyurethane (FPU) has been successfully synthesized and exploited for selective TNP-detection in coexistence with other commonly competitive nitroaromatic analytes. Excellent recognition ability of FPU toward TNP could reach as low as 0.12 μM and the observed fluorescence responses of FPU are exceptional stable to acid, alkali, and salt. Furthermore, a portable, cost effective, and simple indicator paper depending on FPU for visual detection of TNP is developed. FPU indicator paper exhibits two unique advantages: one is the higher selectivity for TNP detection, the other is realizing the rapid and visual explosives detection. The detection mechanism is described for the synergies in the photoelectron transfer (PET), the fluorescence internal filtration effect (IFE) and the fluorescence energy transfer (FRET). This work provides a novel guidance for the design of fluorescent polymers with portable, cost effective, and visual detection of TNP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence detection
Polyurethane
Trinitrophenol
Indicator paper

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a. the FT-IR spectra of NMA, HQ, p-HPMA and FPU; b. the FL spectra of p-HPMA, HQ and FPU; c. the FL spectrum of FPU at different pH. Inset: the FL intensity of FPU(426 nm) at different pH; d. the FL spectra of FPU at different concentrations of NaCl. Inset: the FL intensity of FPU(426 nm) at different concentrations of NaCl.

a. the fluorescence spectrum of FPU with different molecular weight of PEG; b. the fluorescence spectrum of FPU (4 μM) in absolute ethanol containing different concentration of TNP; c. the fitting curve of fluorescence intensity with FPU ethanol solution containing different concentration of TNP; d. the Stern-Volmer curve of FPU ethanol solution containing different concentration of TNP.

a. Comparison of the present approach with other reported methods for the detection of TNP; b. the fluorescence spectrum of FPU with different nitroaromatic compounds (100 μM); c. the 3D Stern-Volmer histogram graph of FPU containing different nitroaromatic compound; d. the △I/I0 histogram of FPU with other commonly competitive nitroaromatic analytes (green) and in coexistence with TNP (red).

a Flowchart of fluorescent indicator paper preparation; b the photos of the FPU indicator paper containing TNP with different concentration; c the photo of FPU indicator paper (I), the photos of the paper without FPU(II,1), the FPU indicator paper with TNP (II, 2), 2,4-DNT (II, 3), NB (II, 4), NT (II, 5), 4-NB (II, 6) and 2,4-DNP (II, 7) solutions (80 μM). All the photos were illuminated by ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm.

a The HOMO and LUMO orbital energies of FPU, TNP, 2,4-DNP, 4-NP, NT, NB and 2,4-DNT; b the UV–vis absorption spectrum of nitroaromatic compound and the excitation spectrum and the emission spectrum of FPU; c The UV–vis spectrum of FPU with different concentration of TNP.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive and portable fluorescence polyurethane indicator paper for real-time, visual and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Superlight Material and Surface Technology of Ministry of Education, College of Material Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Superlight Material and Surface Technology of Ministry of Education
College of Material Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Superlight Material and Surface Technology of Ministry of Education, College of Material Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Visual and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) for public and environmental protection is an extremely urgent issue and challenge. Herein, a novel fluorescence polyurethane (FPU) has been successfully synthesized and exploited for selective TNP-detection in coexistence with other commonly competitive nitroaromatic analytes. Excellent recognition ability of FPU toward TNP could reach as low as 0.12 μM and the observed fluorescence responses of FPU are exceptional stable to acid, alkali, and salt. Furthermore, a portable, cost effective, and simple indicator paper depending on FPU for visual detection of TNP is developed. FPU indicator paper exhibits two unique advantages: one is the higher selectivity for TNP detection, the other is realizing the rapid and visual explosives detection. The detection mechanism is described for the synergies in the photoelectron transfer (PET), the fluorescence internal filtration effect (IFE) and the fluorescence energy transfer (FRET). This work provides a novel guidance for the design of fluorescent polymers with portable, cost effective, and visual detection of TNP.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid and visual detection of harmful explosives is an extremely urgent issue of public security and an important research topic in forensic science [1,2]. 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) is an upgrade explosive product of strong explosive power even at low concentrations (picomolar). Apart from the military application, it can be utilized in fireworks, matchbox factories, dye industries and clinical fields. As industrial leakages and wastes during their production, transportation, usage and storage processes, TNP often causes the significant hazard in ground water and soil because of the high solubility in water. In recent days, various measurement techniques have been established for detection of TNP, such as electrochemical analysis [3,4], energy dispersive X-ray diffraction, spectroscopy [5–7], fluorescence sensing [8,9], capillary electrophoresis. Some disadvantages of high cost of testing, time-consuming, complicated operations and reliance on sophisticated equipment are still needed to be overcame.

Recently, the fluorescence techniques have received considerable attentions due to their simplicity, rapid and low cost [10–12]. For example, a novel dual-emitting dye-CDs@MOFs was prepared for selective and sensitive identification of antibiotics and MnO4 − in water, as reported by Fan et al. [13]. To data, the fluorescent materials, such as MOFs [14–16], quantum dots [17–19], polymers [20–23], organic compounds [24,25], have been designed to fluorescence analysis and used in the explosives detection. However, portable, real-time and visual selective detection of TNP is still a challenge.

Polyurethanes (PUs) as a common polymer which applied in the fields of coatings [26], elastomers [27], foams [28], leathers [29]. Compared with traditional organic fluorescence materials, PUs offer advantages in terms of facile chemical preparation, large scale production and low cost. However, the fluorescence property is often overlooked. PUs is electron rich polymer. According the mechanism of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [30], electron rich PUs could be quenched by readily-accessible nitro-aromatic compounds (electron-deficient compounds). In this work, we designed a hydroquinone acrylamide to prepare the fluorescence polyurethane and indicator paper, which provides new sights on developing an efficient monitoring method for real-time, visual and selective detecting TNP. Fluorescence PUs indicator exhibits two unique advantages: one is the higher selectivity for TNP detection, the other is realizing the visual explosives detection. The detection mechanism for TNP via fluorescence polyurethane indicator, such as the photoelectron transfer (PET), the fluorescence internal filtration effect (IFE) and the fluorescence energy transfer (FRET), were discussed, respectively.

Hydroquinone (HQ), N-hydroxymethyl acrylamide (NMA), tetrahydrofuran (THF), ether polyethylene glycol (PEG), hexamethylene diisocyanate (HDI) and 1,4-diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane (DABCO) were purchased from Aladdin reagent corporation (China). Picric acid (TNP), nitrobenzene (NB), 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), p-nitrophenol (NP), 4-nitrotoluene (NT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT) were all from Shanghai Hongshun Biological Technology Co., LTD. All reagents were used without further purification.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR, Spectrum 100) spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer spectrometer (China) at room temperature and KBr was used as the sample matrix. All of the spectra were acquired at a resolution of 4 cm−1 with eight scans. 1H NMR spectra were measured on a 500 MHz NMR (Bruker AVANCE, United Kingdom) at room temperature. The UV–vis spectra was conducted using Shimadzu spectrometer (Shanghai, China) from 200 nm to 600 nm, with a resolution of 1 nm and a quartz plate as the substrate. Fluorescence measurements were performed using the LS55 fluorescence photometer (Shanghai, China). The width of the excitation and emission slit was 10 nm and the scanning rate was 200 nm/min.

The preparation route is shown in Fig. 1 . The synthesize of N,N′-[(2,5-dihydroxy-1,4-phenylene)bis(methylene)]bisacrylamide (p-HPMA) is the Friedel-Crafts alkylation reaction according to a recent literature [31]. HQ and NMA (1:2) with 100 mL absolute alcohol were mixed into 250 mL three-neck flask. After the mixtures were dissolved, 10 mL concentrated sulfuric acid was slowly dropped into the solution under stirring. The solution was stirred for 4 days at room temperature. The product was obtained with 85% yield after extraction and recrystallization.

The synthesize route of fluorescence polyurethane (FPU) is shown in Fig. 1. PEG (0.59 g), p-HPMA (0.72 g), THF (24 mL), HDI (0.91 g) and DABCO (0.18 g) were added into a dry bottle one by one. The solution was heated to 75 °C under the atmosphere of N2 for 8 h. The clear solution became markedly viscous. The product was precipitated from excess ether. The product was obtained after 24 h drying and the yield was 70.5%.

The filter paper was immersed to the FPU solution (DMSO/ethanol solution, V/V = 1:10) for 10 min. Then the filter paper was transferred to the oven at 60 °C for 30 min. The indicator paper was obtained after cooled to the room temperature and could be cut to any sizes.

A certain of FPU was dissolved in the solvent of DMSO under sonication for 20 min. The solution was diluted to 4 μM by absolute alcohol for the fluorescence test. To value the stable of fluorescence, the solution was tested after prepared 1 hour and 7 days, respectively.

The solution of nitroaromatic were prepared by absolute alcohol including TNP, NB, 2,4-DNP, NP, NT and 2,4 DNT, respectively. The solution of nitroaromatic and 8 μM of PU were mixed (1:1 V/V) before tested and the concentration of nitroaromatic compounds was from 10 μM to 100 μM.

(1) Calculation of fluorescence quenching (%):
F Q =
I 0
− I
I 0
×
100 %
where FQ is the fluorescence quenching percentage, I0 is the initial fluorescence intensity without analyte, I is the fluorescence intensity corresponding analyte.

(2) Calculation of the Stern-Volmer quenching constant:
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
S V
×
[ C ]
where I0 is the initial fluorescence intensity without analyte, I is the fluorescence intensity corresponding analyte, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [C] is the concentration of analyte.

(3) Calculation of detection limit
D L =
3 σ
K
where DL is the detection limit, σ is the standard deviation for the solution intensity of FPU with analyte, K is the slope of the curve.

The structures of p-HPMA and FPU were confirmed by the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 a) and 1HNMR spectra (Figure S1). From the black curve (NMA) in Fig. 2a, it's clearly to be seen that the peak at 3376 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of hydrogen bonded NH. The characteristic peak of hydrogen bonded OH is shown at 3270 cm−1. The peak at 3038 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of hydrogen bonded C = C H. The peaks at 1648 cm−1 and 1606 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of C = O (amides) and C = C, respectively. The stretching vibration of CO is at 1214 cm−1. The red curve in Fig. 2a is the FT-IR of HQ. The broad band at 3234 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH. Multiple peaks of the benzene ring are observed at 1604, 1500 and 1456 cm−1. The peaks at 3328, 3248, 3059, 1636, 1583, 1510 and 1450 cm−1 from the green line in Fig. 2a are evidences of NH, OH, C = C H, C = O and benzene ring, respectively. These peaks in green line are also found in the blue line (FPU) in Fig. 2a, indicating that the structure of FPU contained p-HPMA. Except those peaks, the bands at 2938 and 2852 cm−1 from the blue line (FPU) in Fig. 2a are attributed to the stretching vibration of methyl or methylene group. The peak at 1708 cm−1 is from the stretch vibration of C = O (ester group).

The 1H NMR spectra of p-HMPA and FPU are shown in Figure S1. The 1H NMR of p-HMPA (500 MHz, DMSO‑d6 , δ[ppm]): 8.89 (1H, s), 8.51 (1H, t), 6.66 (1H, s), 6.29 (1H, q), 6.14 (1H, q), 5.64 (1H, q), 4.25 (2H, d). The 1H NMR of FPU (500 MHz, DMSO‑d6, δ[ppm]):6.14 (1H, m), 5.72–5.63 (2H, m), 4.29 (3H, t), 4.24 (6H, d), 4.05 (2H, s), 3.37 (3H, t), 2.96 (1H, s), 1.39 (1H, s), 1.24 (1H, d), 1.01 (2H, s). The peak at 2.96 in the 1H NMR of FPU belongs to the O = C NH of FPU. From the above, p-HPMA and FPU were successful synthesized as followed by the steps of Fig. 1.

The fluorescence performance of FPU was investigated by the fluorescence (FL) spectrum. The test samples were easily dissolved in DMSO/ethanol solution (V/V = 1/10) and the concentration of test samples were 4 μM. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensity of FPU is obvious stronger than that of p-HPMA and HQ. This is because the structure of FPU possess good conjugation. After the conjugated system of FPU absorbed the UV light, the electron transfer (π→π*) occurred to emit fluorescence. The locally excited state and charge transfer state of FPU may emit double peaks in the fluorescence emission peak (401 nm and 426 nm).

To study the stability of FPU in different environments, the fluorescence intensity of FPU solution from acidic to alkaline (pH=3~11) were measured and the results are shown in Fig. 2c. The fluorescence intensity of FPU (426 nm) hardly changed from acidic to alkaline, indicating that the fluorescence responds of FPU have good acid and alkali resistance. In addition, the Fig. 2d shows the fluorescence intensity of FPU (426 nm) with different concentrations of NaCl solution (from 0.5 M to 3 M). It can be seen clearly that the fluorescence responds of FPU in different concentrations of NaCl environment are almost same, illustrating that FPU has good salt resistance. The stability of FPU provides potential possibilities for its application under complex and harsh conditions.

To investigate the influence of PEG with different molecular weight for FPU, the molecular weight from 200 to 6000 of PEG were used to synthesize FPU. The results of FT-IR in Figure S2 indicates that the structures of FPU unchanged with different molecular weight of PEG. The fluorescence spectrum was also measured and the results shows in Fig. 3 a. At the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, FPU showed strong green emission fluorescence in DMSO/ethanol solution (V/V = 1:10, 4 μM), and had a strong fluorescence peak at 426 nm. The fluorescence intensity of FPU increased with the increasing of the molecular weight of PEG. However, when the molecular weight of PEG is too large (over 2000), the steric hindrance of the polymer weakens the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity of FPU with PEG-2000 is obviously stronger than that of others, so the FPU with PEG-2000 was chosen to detect the nitroaromatic compounds.

To study the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of FPU toward TNP, the fluorescence spectrum of FPU with different concentration of TNP are shown in Fig. 3b. It's obviously to been seen that the fluorescence intensity of FPU became weaker and weaker as the addition of TNP, indicating that FPU could be used to real-time detect TNP. The fluorescence quenching rate is over 90% when the concentration of TNP was 50 μM. The fitting curve of fluorescence intensity with FPU ethanol solution containing different concentration of TNP is shown in Fig. 3c and the slope of the curve is 11.592. Depending on the formula (3), the detection limit of FPU for TNP is 0.12 μM, which is lower than other previous reports [32–41] in Fig. 4 a (Table S1). The Stern-Volmer curve of fluorescence intensity with FPU ethanol solution containing different concentration of TNP in Figure S3. The Stern-Volmer curve tended to upward at high concentration because of the super quenching effect. At the low concentration of TNP, the Stern-Volmer curve exhibited good linear relationship (Fig. 3d) and the calculation result of quenching constant (KSV ) based on the formula (2) is 5.9 × 104 M − 1.

To investigate the selective performace of FPU to TNP, five kinds nitroaromatic compound (NB, NT, 2,4-DNT, 2,4-DNP and 4-NP) were used to compare. The fluorescence intensity of FPU solution containing different nitroaromatic compound at the same concentration of 100 μM are shown in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence intensity order is TNP > 2,4-DNP > 4-NP > NT > NB>2, 4-DNT. The detection limits of FPU to TNP, 2,4-DNP, 4-NP, NT, NB and 2,4-DNT are 0.12 μM, 0.56 μM, 0.77 μM, 0.89 μM, 1.01 μM, 1.18 μM, respectively. The 3D Stern-Volmer histogram graph of FPU containing different nitroaromatic compound is shown in Fig. 4b. The nitroaromatic compound could quench the fluorescence of FPU to some extent. The quenching ability of TNP is the strongest among the nitroaromatic compounds at the same concentration from the Fig. 4c, indicating the incomparable sensitive of FPU to TNP. The △I/I0 values of FPU with 2,4-DNP, 4-NP, NT, NB and 2,4-DNT (green histograms in Fig. 4d) are significantly lower than that of the addition of TNP (red histograms in Fig. 4d). These results clearly demonstrate FPU has the unprecedented selective and sensitive for reorganization of TNP containing potential interfering nitroaromatic compounds.

Considering the simplicity and rapidity of fluorescence sensor for explosive detection, a portable indicator paper for visual detection of TNP was developed (Fig. 5 a). The filter paper was immersed to the FPU solution (DMSO/ethanol solution, v/v = 1:10) for 10 min. After drying, the green fluorescent indicator paper under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp was obtained. The quenching performance of the indicator paper was also evaluated by adding the different concentrations of TNP solution (Fig. 5b). With the increase of TNP concentration, the fluorescence quenching was becoming more and more obvious. Meanwhile, after adding the same concentration of TNP, 2,4-DNP, 4-NP, NT, NB and 2,4-DNT, the fluorescence of FPU indicator papers were observed under the ultraviolet radiation of 365 nm (Fig. 5c). Only TNP quenched the fluorescence of FPU indicator paper. All these results show that the indicator paper provides a convenient fluorescence platform for TNP detection with high sensitivity and selectivity.

In order to verify whether electron transfer occurs between electron-rich FPU and electron-short nitroaromatic, LUMO and HOMO orbitals were calculated (Fig. 6 a). When the conduction band energy level of the ligand is higher than the LUMO orbital energy level of nitrocompound, electron transfer is a factor leading to fluorescence quenching. The HOMO and LUMO orbital energies of FPU are −5.27 eV and −8.57 eV, respectively. The LUMO of TNP (−6.31 eV) is significantly lower than that of FPU, indicating the electron transfer from the excited state of FPU to the electron-lacking TNP. Therefore, the high sensitivity and selectivity of FPU to TNP can be attributed to the mechanism of PET. In addition, the LUMO energy level of TNP is significantly lower than that of other nitrocompounds, so its quenching effect is most obvious among other nitrocompounds, which is consistent with the experimental results (Fig. 4c). However, for other nitro compounds, the quenching effect is not completely consistent with the orbital energy level, this is because that PET is not the only detection mechanism for TNP's highly selective fluorescence quenching.

IFE is one of the fluorescence quenching mechanism, which can be described that the electrons is more easily transferred from fluorescer to quencher as the largest of the overlap area between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the quencher and the excitation spectrum (Ex) of the fluorescer, resulting the obviously fluorescence quenching effect. The excitation spectrum of FPU overlaps the UV–vis absorption spectrum of all nitroaromatic compound to some extent in Fig. 6(b), resulting all nitroaromatic compound could quench the fluorescence of FPU. This is consistent with the conclusion the experimental results in Fig. 4(c).

FRET is also one of the fluorescence quenching mechanism, which can be explained that the resonance energy is more easily transferred from fluorescer to quencher as the largest of the overlap area between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the quencher and the emission spectrum (Em) of the fluorescer, resulting the obviously fluorescence quenching effect. As shown in Fig. 6(b), only the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TNP overlaps the emission spectrum of FPU, illustrating that the selectivity and sensitivity of FPU to TNP. The UV–vis spectrum of FPU (Fig. 6c) shows that with the increase of TNP concentration, the absorption intensity gradually increases without causing any shift of the FPU peak, which indicates that it is impossible to form a ground-state charge complex between FPU and TNP.

To sum up, the quenching effect of TNP to FPU is attributed to the synergies in PET, IFE and FRET. However, are there other quenching mechanism occurring? The further research of quenching mechanism of TNP to FPU are still needed.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized fluorescence polyurethane, which exhibits green emission fluorescence at 426 nm. Fluorescence studies demonstrate that FPU is selective and sensitive for reorganization of TNP containing potential interfering nitroaromatic compounds. The observed fluorescence responses of FPU toward TNP are stable to acid, alkali, and salt. High quenching effect of FPU upon addition of TNP is attributed to the synergies in PET, IFE and FRET. The preparation method of FPU indicator paper is easy and provides a novel stage for portable, cost effective, and visual detection of TNP.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51803041), the Fundamental Research Funds of the Central University (No. 3072019CFM1000), the Projects of State Key Laboratory of Marine Coatings. Dr. Shan Gao and Ms. Li Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ceja.2020.100069.
